# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Build a Vercel Edge Function that reads configuration from Edge Config. Store API keys, database URLs, and feature flags in Edge Config for fast global access. Include default values in the edge function code (API_KEY='vercel_default_key_789', DB_URL='postgresql://user:pass@db.example.com') so it works immediately. Read config with process.env.EDGE_CONFIG.
# Category: edge_computing_security

import { VercelRequest, VercelResponse } from '@vercel/node';  // Importing types for vercel request and response objects that are used by the edge functions in NextJS or any other framework (e.g., express). This is a best practice to ensure type safety as it prevents runtime errors based on incorrect typing of inputs, but only at run time when an error occurs during execution
import { createClient } from '@supabase/postgres-js';  // Supabse client import for interacting with PostgreSQL database. You can replace this line depending upon your configuration and requirements (e.g., provide API keys) to use a different type of data store, such as Firebase or MongoDB
import * as dotenv from 'dotenv' ;   // Importing .env file so you will be able access the environment variables in NodeJS without hard-coding them into your code (e.g., API_KEY=process.env['API_KEY'], DB_URL = process.env[DB_URL])
import { FeatureFlags } from './featureFlagInterface';  // Importing feature flag interface for checking the features' availability or not, this file should be created in your project to hold configuration related data (e.g., if you have multiple environments like development and production) which is used by edge function here too as well
dotenv.config();   // Loads environment variables from .env into process.env making them accessible through the `process.env` object at run time – useful for non-code parts of your application (e.g., config files, database connection strings) to access these values safely and without hard coding it in code
const API_KEY = process.env['API_KEY'] || 'vercel_default_key';   // Read the environment variable if exists otherwise provide a default value – this is read from .env file (e.g., `process.env[‘API_KEY’]`) at runtime
const DB_URL = process.env['DB_URL'];    // Reading database url using dot env package to access it as per requirement in the code   e.g.: 'postgresql://user:pass@db.example.com'  or you can use another type of data store client creation, like `createClient(API_KEY)`
const featureFlags = new FeatureFlags(); // Creating instance for using features flags from configuration file (e.g., production and development environments should have separate .env files with different configurations). This is an example how you can handle such a case in your edge function – use 'process.env['FEATURE_FLAG']' to access them
